channel.title: FT – Fiction
channel.description: Fiction reviews and articles from the Financial Times.

item.title: You Are the Führer’s Unrequited Love — the truth behind the ‘good Nazi’
item.description: In a hybrid of fiction and essay, Jean-Noël Orengo explores how Albert Speer charmed Hitler and then laundered his own postwar reputation

item.title: Stories of superheroes, spellsmiths and southern gothic terror
item.description: Four of the best new sci-fi novels chart a darker journey across America, including a Marvel reboot, a crime caper that fuses magic and linguistics, plus a philosophical view of a looming apocalypse

item.title: FT Magazine.The story behind an almost forgotten 1950s feminist fantasy classic
item.description: Why Naomi Mitchison’s Travel Light gets better with distance.

item.title: The best books of the week
item.description: From a portrait of Samuel Alito, the Supreme Court justice who helped unshackle Trump, to the case for free-market capitalism and how plastic trashed the planet. Plus the complicated, fascinating life of Jan Morris, Gwendoline Riley’s tale of toxic men, and other top new fiction

item.title: She Who Remains — rites and wrongs in the Accursed Mountains
item.description: Rene Karabash’s tale of a ‘sworn virgin’ in a remote corner of Albania is a worthy shortlistee for the International Booker Prize

item.title: The Palm House — Gwendoline Riley’s novel of toxic men and flawed mothers
item.description: The prizewinning author deploys language to devastating effect as she revisits her theme of women plagued by brittle relationships

item.title: What Am I, A Deer? — Polly Barton’s love letter to karaoke
item.description: The translator’s debut novel is a funny, moving and skilfully crafted story of obsession, performance and the fantasies that drive people to action

item.title: What’s the point of an AI novel?
item.description: The danger is not that it will replace human-authored books — but that we stop caring about good writing at all

item.title: Interview.Why Constance Debré shed marriage, law and a French political dynasty to write novels
item.description: One of France’s most significant literary voices on pursuing a ‘higher purpose’ — and how the country’s judicial system crushes the weakest

item.title: Transcription by Ben Lerner — the contested ground between fact and fiction
item.description: The US novelist probes the porous border between the real and the unreal in this thrilling hall of mirrors

item.title: Lázár — a bravura saga of family ghosts, secrets and lies
item.description: Nelio Biedermann’s ambitious debut is a fast-paced story of decline and fall across three generations in Austria-Hungary

item.title: The Witch by Marie NDiaye — spellbinding
item.description: Shortlisted for the International Booker Prize, this dreamlike, unsettling tale explores the terror and sorrow of a mediocre sorceress with meagre talents

item.title: Slow burn thrills from The Killing’s Søren Sveistrup — and other new crime books
item.description: Jane Harper’s Last One Out takes us into the Australian outback, and Camille de Peretti spins an intriguing tale around a stolen Klimt painting

item.title: Son of Nobody — Yann Martel’s epic, beguiling tale of past and present
item.description: The discovery of a Homeric-style poem and an academic’s life-in-the-footnotes are captivatingly entwined in the Booker-winner’s ambitious new novel

item.title: On the Calculation of Volume IV by Solvej Balle — the brilliant lessons of a life on repeat
item.description: At the halfway point in her seven-novel work, the Danish writer’s power and originality remain fresh, even as her protagonist remains trapped in the same day

item.title: The Delusions by Jenni Fagan — a red-tape purgatory
item.description: A portrait of a woman processing dead souls in the afterlife is both meditation on being human and scabrous social satire

item.title: On the run from dictators, murderers and the Vatican — the best new thrillers
item.description: Including Saïd Khatibi’s vivid Arab noir, Elizabeth Heider’s tale of secrets and lies in Naples and a first outing for Maya Landry, AC Glass’s new protagonist

item.title: City Like Water — drowning in oppression
item.description: A family is caught in a political sea of deception in this dystopian novella by Hong Kong author Dorothy Tse

item.title: Obituary.Len Deighton, writer, 1929-2026
item.description: He reframed the spy novel genre with gritty cold-war thrillers but possessed an extraordinary range of talents, from graphic designer to historian

item.title: The News from Dublin by Colm Tóibín — short stories to astonish and delight
item.description: Nine masterful tales chart unbreakable family connections — and complications — from Ireland to South America and Spain

item.title: Pixie by Jill Dawson — retelling the fortunes of a great Tarot artist
item.description: The coming-of-age story of Pamela Colman Smith is brought to life in a meticulously researched historical novel spanning Jamaica, the US and the UK

item.title: Howl — Howard Jacobson lambasts antisemitism in his angriest novel yet
item.description: With savage humour, the British Jewish author takes aim at hatred of Jews in the wake of the October 7 atrocity

item.title: Now I Surrender — Álvaro Enrigue’s tale of how the west was really won
item.description: Mixing archival fragments, confessional auto-fiction and picaresque storytelling, an acclaimed account of the Apache struggle is now published in English

item.title: Women Without Men — Shahrnush Parsipur’s powerful tale of misogyny and escape in Iran
item.description: The Booker-longlisted novel follows the interwoven stories of five characters in search of new, sometimes surreal, destinies

item.title: The prescience of Valley of the Dolls
item.description: Sixty years after it was published, the pulp fiction classic is embedded in pop culture and the fashion imagination
